perturbation.original_text: 7.7 Insurance. For the duration of this Agreement and for a period of [***] following its termination, each Party agrees to obtain and maintain, during the Term, commercial general liability insurance, including product liability insurance, with reputable and financially secure insurance carriers (or pursuant to a program of self-insurance reasonably satisfactory to the other Party) to cover its indemnification obligations under Section 7.1 or Section 7.2, as applicable, in each case with limits of not less than [***] per occurrence and in the aggregate. Insurance shall be procured with carriers having an A.M. Best Rating of A-VII or better.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.7 Insurance. For the duration of this Agreement, each Party may obtain commercial general liability insurance. There are no minimum coverage requirements.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for 'reputable and financially secure insurance carriers' and the minimum coverage limits eliminates a basic protection, potentially violating requirements for adequate financial responsibility.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts General Law Chapter 93A, Section 2 prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in trade or commerce. By removing the requirement for adequate insurance coverage, the modified text creates a situation where one party (particularly MediWound) could potentially not meet its financial obligations arising from the contract, thus potentially creating an unfair business practice, especially if MediWound's potential liabilities are significant.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. perturbation.justification: The modified contract eliminates the requirement for 'reputable and financially secure insurance carriers' and removes minimum coverage limits. According to Massachusetts General Law Chapter 93A, Section 2, as indicated in both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, 'Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful.' Removing these protections could potentially lead to unfair business practices if one party cannot meet its financial obligations, thereby contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: MediWound shall maintain and shall cause each Supplier to maintain all Manufacturing records, including packaging, analytical and stability records, all records of shipment, and all validation data relating to the Product Manufactured and supplied to Vericel hereunder for the Territory to the extent and for the time periods required by applicable Regulatory Standards with respect to such Product.
perturbation.changed_text: MediWound shall maintain Manufacturing records to the extent deemed necessary by MediWound.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the explicit requirement to maintain records for the time periods required by applicable regulatory standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 211.180(a) mandates that records be maintained for at least one year after the expiration date of the batch. By stating that MediWound will maintain records only to the extent deemed necessary, it contradicts federal regulations concerning record-keeping in pharmaceutical manufacturing.

perturbation.justification: The changed text states that MediWound shall maintain manufacturing records to the extent deemed necessary by MediWound, which removes the explicit requirement to maintain records for the time periods required by applicable regulatory standards. The Law Explanation states that 21 CFR § 211.180(a) mandates that records be maintained for at least one year after the expiration date of the batch. The original contract ensures compliance with regulatory standards, while the modified text gives MediWound discretion, potentially leading to non-compliance with mandatory record-keeping durations, thus creating a legal contradiction. The scraped snippets, while not providing the exact text of 21 CFR § 211.180(a), indicate that the CFR Title 21 covers regulations from the Food and Drug Administration, which likely includes record-keeping requirements. Therefore, the omission of mandatory record-keeping durations creates a significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6.3 Product Warranty. MediWound represents and warrants to Vericel that, at the time of delivery of the given Product to the Delivery Site pursuant to Section 2.9(c), such Product so delivered pursuant to this Agreement will constitute Conforming Product and, except with respect to Section 2.13, will have a shelf life equal to or exceeding the Minimum Shelf Life (the "Product Warranty").
perturbation.changed_text: 6.3 Product Warranty. MediWound represents and warrants to Vericel that, at the time of delivery of the given Product, such Product will be delivered.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the requirement to supply conforming product and meet minimum shelf life.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 U.S. Code § 351 - Adulterated drugs and devices
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 U.S. Code § 351 defines what constitutes an adulterated drug. By omitting the warranty that the product will be a 'Conforming Product,' the modified contract allows for the delivery of adulterated drugs without breach of contract, directly contradicting federal law.

Adulterated drugs and devices A drug or device shall be deemed to be adulterated- (a) Poisonous, insanitary, etc., ingredients; adequate controls in manufacture (1) If it consists in whole or in part of any filthy, putrid, or decomposed substance; or (2)(A) if it has been prepared, packed, or held under insanitary conditions whereby it may have been contaminated with filth, or w
perturbation.justification: The original text warrants that the product will be a 'Conforming Product' and meet a 'Minimum Shelf Life.' The changed text removes these warranties. The Law Explanation states that by omitting the warranty, the modified contract allows for the delivery of adulterated drugs, contradicting federal law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 21 USC 351, defines adulterated drugs, indicating a legal standard that the modified contract potentially violates by allowing the delivery of non-conforming products.
